title: Hillary's Locker Cabinet with Charging Cubbies
summary: A face-framed locker cabinet with a bank of build-to-fit drawers and a charging cubby wired through a drilled back panel.
description:
This is a real face-framed cabinet, which means every joint is reinforced by the frame itself rather than relying on the box joints alone — get the box built square from the start and the drawers will slide the way they're supposed to. The drawer faces are sized off your actual 1x8 boards rather than a fixed number: measure your boards' real width, add 1/4" for drawer-side allowance, and cut the cubby openings to that number. Lumber runs a little different width from batch to batch, and building to your own material instead of a spec sheet is what makes drawers that fit.

The drawers themselves are cut-to-fit, not cut-to-spec — build each one slightly oversized, then trim it down to its own cubby opening (about 1/8" clearance per side for a first attempt, tighter if you're confident in your cuts), because no two openings in a hand-built cabinet are perfectly identical. This is also a charging station: one drawer gets a hole drilled through the back panel to route a cable through. Because this is a tall, top-heavy cabinet, it must be secured to a wall stud, not left freestanding — that's not optional trim, it's what keeps it from tipping.

tools.note: Alternative to countersunk screws or nails for the carcass joints.
Drills the cable pass-through hole in the back panel for the charging cubby.

materials.name: Wall anchors or lag screws (to secure to a stud)
Candle wax
materials.note: Rubbed on drawer bottoms after finishing for a smooth slide.

cutList.note: Splits the cabinet into the locker side and the drawer cubby side.
Measure your 1x8 boards' actual width and cut these to that width plus 1/4" for drawer allowance.
Cut to fit — treat this as a starting size and trim each drawer to its own opening.
Height matches your actual 1x8 board width — cut to fit each opening.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: Cut the 1x12s first, because the layout is tight: two sides and one main divider at 75-1/4", four top-and-bottom pieces at 25-1/2", one fixed shelf at 36-1/4", one bottom shelf at 27", and four cubby dividers at 7-1/2". Take each 75-1/4" piece off its own board. Be aware before you start that those parts total 421" of 1x12 while the declared lumber — three 8 ft boards and one 10 ft — comes to 408", so buy one more 8 ft 1x12 rather than discovering the shortfall at the last shelf. From the 1x2s cut two frame stiles at 73-3/4", two rails at 27" and a centre rail at 24". From the single 1x6 cut six drawer sides at 10-1/2" and three drawer fronts and backs at 5-3/4", treating both as starting sizes only. Hold the cubby dividers until you have measured your actual 1x8 — their 7-1/2" is your board's real width plus the 1/4" drawer allowance, and the 7-1/4" drawer face in the cut list is that same board width.

steps.title: Build the box square
steps.body:
If you get this box built square and right, your drawers will slide great — take your time, mark all your joints, and check for square at least twice. Measure the actual width of your 1x8 boards to get your drawer face heights, add 1/4" to that number, and cut your cubby dividers to that width for well-fitting drawers.

If you don't have a pocket-hole jig, add the main divider (next step) before assembling the box — you won't be able to fit a drill in there afterward.

steps.title: Add the main divider
steps.body: The main divider is a full-height 75-1/4" 1x12 that splits the cabinet into the tall locker bay on one side and the stack of drawer cubbies on the other. Mark its position on both the top and the bottom panel before you fasten anything — the top and bottom are 25-1/2" and the divider takes 3/4" out of that, so the two bays share what is left, and a divider that leans throws every cubby opening out of square. Fasten with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws and glue, or with countersunk screws or finish nails and glue if you skipped the jig. Check it square to the bottom panel in both directions before the glue grabs.

steps.title: Add sides, top, and bottom shelf
steps.body: Add the two 75-1/4" sides and the 25-1/2" top and bottom panels around the divider, working on a flat floor. The cabinet finishes 27" wide overall, which is what both the 27" back panel and the 27" face-frame rails confirm. Fasten with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws and glue, or countersunk screws or finish nails and glue — the face frame added later reinforces every one of these joints, so they do not have to carry the cabinet on their own. Drop the 27" bottom shelf and the 36-1/4" fixed shelf into their marked positions while the box is still open and easy to reach. Measure both diagonals of the front opening one last time and adjust until they match, because everything about the drawers depends on it.

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body: Cut the 1/4" plywood back 27" by 76" — the cabinet's full outside footprint — and mark the position of every fixed shelf on the outside of it first, so your nails land in wood rather than in air. Glue and nail it to the sides, top, bottom and each fixed shelf with 1-1/4" finish nails. Tack one corner, confirm the diagonals still match, then nail the rest off at roughly 6" spacing. On a cabinet this tall the back is the only thing resisting racking, so do not leave it off.

steps.title: Attach the face frame
steps.body: Build the face frame flat on a table before it goes anywhere near the cabinet. The two 27" rails run the full width and the 73-3/4" stiles sit between them — that is the only reading that closes, since 73-3/4" plus 3/4" of rail at each end gives exactly the 75-1/4" side length. The 24" centre rail crosses between the stiles wherever the locker bay meets the drawer cubbies. Join with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws and glue, check the frame square on its own, then glue it to the front of the carcass and pin with 1-1/4" finish nails, keeping the outside edges flush with the cabinet sides.

steps.title: Add top and bottom trim
steps.body: Wrap the base of the cabinet with 1x2 trim and add a matching band at the top. There are no cut-list rows for this trim, so measure the assembled front and sides and cut every piece to your own piece — the three listed 1x2 boards are already consumed by the face frame (two 73-3/4" stiles and the rails), so buy at least one extra 8 ft 1x2 for the base and crown trim before you start cutting it. Miter the two front corners at 45°, cutting the front piece first and the side returns against it, and dry-fit all three before any glue. Pin with 1-1/4" finish nails into the carcass edges.

steps.title: Add the crown
steps.body: Cap the top with a final 1x2 band standing slightly proud of the trim below, again cut to fit rather than to a number — the plan gives no dimensions for it. Miter the two front corners at 45° and dry-fit all three pieces together before any glue, adjusting the miters until they close with no gap. On a cabinet 75-1/4" tall this joint sits at eye level and an open miter is the first thing anyone will see. Pin with 1-1/4" finish nails and fill the joints before finishing.

steps.title: Build the drawers
steps.body:
The trick with these drawers is building each one to fit its own opening. For a first attempt, leave about 1/8" clearance on each side; if you're confident in your cuts, 1/16" gives a tighter fit. Tack the plywood bottom onto each drawer box. For the charging drawer, drill a hole through the back panel to route a cable through.

Each drawer opening may be slightly different, so build and fit each drawer to its own cubby rather than to a single measurement. Rub candle wax on the drawer bottoms after finishing for the smoothest slide.

steps.title: Install the drawers and faces
steps.body: Slide each drawer box into its cubby and confirm it runs without binding before any face goes on. Cut the three drawer faces from the 1x8 — each is your board's full width by 7-1/4", and all three come out of the 2 ft piece with room for the saw kerfs. Hold each face over its drawer with an even reveal all round, tack it from the outside with two 1-1/4" finish nails, then pull the drawer and drive the rest from the inside so no nail head shows on the front. Drill the knob hole through the face and the drawer front together in one pass so it cannot wander.

steps.title: Add hooks and secure to the wall
steps.body: Lay out the three coat hooks across the locker bay, spacing them evenly and setting them high enough that a hanging coat clears the fixed shelf below. Then secure the cabinet before anything goes in it: at 75-1/4" tall, narrow, and heaviest at the top once the locker fills, it will tip. Find the stud behind it, drill through the back panel and into the stud, and fix it with lag screws or wall anchors. This is not optional trim — it is what keeps the cabinet from coming down on someone.
materials: Wall anchors or lag screws (to secure to a stud)

images.alt: Locker cabinet with charging cubbies and build-to-fit drawers
